interactions.summary: Jiang turns Salamis and Plataea into a lesson about glory overriding strategy: Xerxes and then Mardonius abandon easy attrition, take unnecessary head-on battles, and turn a war Persia should have won into disaster.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang lecture voice, paraphrasing Persian commanders.

interactions.summary: After the Persian defeat, Greece becomes wealthy and Athens creates the Delian League as a defensive naval alliance funded by allies' money and Athenian ships.

He introduces Pericles against the worshipful historian image: democracy, popular voting, public spending, moving Delian funds to Athens, the Parthenon, and officialized corruption are framed as techniques for amassing power.

speaker_notes.note: Continuous Jiang lecture; historical speech by Xerxes, generals, and Pericles is paraphrased for explanation.

claims.claim: Jiang argues Xerxes chose Salamis from a desire for remembered greatness over the safer strategy of starving out the Greeks.
The lecture frames Salamis as the point where the Persian army had already won the war but the Persian navy lost it by fighting in a narrow strait against heavier Greek ships.
Jiang says Persia could still have won through attrition after Salamis, but Mardonius repeated the mistake by fighting an unnecessary equal-force battle at Plataea.
claims.temporal_scope: Counterfactual strategic argument in the 2024-10-15 lecture.

claims.claim: After the Persian wars, Athens organizes the Delian League as a naval alliance in which Athens provides ships while allied islands and colonies provide money reserved for war against Persia.
Jiang presents Pericles as a politician concerned with amassing, increasing, and keeping power, despite acknowledging that historians treat him as a great democratic leader.
Pericles' expansion of democracy is interpreted as a lower-nobility strategy: align with the people to defeat upper-nobility prestige and money, effectively making himself king of Athens.
Jiang calls Pericles' transfer and spending of Delian League money official corruption because it turned allied funds into Athenian jobs, monuments, and patronage.

signature_moments.moment: Xerxes rejects slow victory because he wants one great battle that history will remember.
signature_moments.source_phrase: I want one great battle so that history will remember me forever
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The heroic desire for memory becomes a strategic poison, connecting Persian failure back to the eudaimonia problem.

signature_moments.moment: At Salamis, Persia wins the land war and loses the war in the same motion.
signature_moments.source_phrase: The Persian army had won the war. And at Salamis, they lost the war.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It condenses the lecture's logic of self-defeat through unnecessary glory-seeking battle.

signature_moments.moment: Pericles makes corruption official by moving the allied treasury to Athens and spending it as public splendor.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It flips the democratic monument story into a patronage and power story.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Waiting out an enemy because the enemy has fewer resources.
A defensive alliance against Persia in which Athens supplied the navy and allies supplied money stored at Delos.
Athenian leader from 461 to 429 BCE, treated by Jiang as both democratic icon and power-seeking politician.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR renders Plataea as 'Palatia'; extraction preserves the historical referent but does not rewrite transcript text.
